1:0.rendering: I said in my heart, "Come now, I will test you with pleasure. So enjoy yourself." But look, this too was vapour.
1:0.note: The Hebrew word for "pleasure" here echoes the garden's delights in Genesis, yet the Preacher finds it as fleeting as breath.

1:1.rendering: I said of laughter, "It is madness," and of pleasure, "What does it accomplish?"
1:1.note: The Hebrew word for "madness" here echoes the book's opening verdict that "all is vapour" under the sun, exposing even royal merriment as empty wind.

1:2.rendering: I searched in my heart to cheer my flesh with wine, while guiding my heart with wisdom, and to lay hold on folly, until I could see what was good for the children of humanity under the heavens during the few days of their lives.
1:2.note: The Hebrew word for "search" here echoes the king's quest in 1 Kings 3 for wisdom, but now it's twisted into an experiment mixing pleasure and insight.

1:3.rendering: I built myself houses, I planted vineyards for myself.
1:3.note: The Hebrew piles up "for myself" five times in verses 4 through 8, exposing the self-centered quest for meaning under the sun.

1:4.rendering: I made myself gardens and parks, and I planted in them every kind of fruit tree.
1:4.note: The Hebrew word for "parks" (pardes) is a Persian loanword, evoking the lush royal gardens of ancient empires that the Preacher imitated in his quest for meaning.

1:5.rendering: I made pools of water for myself, to water from them the forest sprouting with trees.
1:5.note: The Hebrew word for "pools" echoes the reservoirs of Egypt and the lush gardens of ancient kings, exposing how Solomon's building spree mimicked the very empires he was meant to surpass.

1:6.rendering: I bought male and female slaves, and I had slaves born in my house. I also had great possessions of herds and flocks, more than all who were in Jerusalem before me.
1:6.note: The Hebrew word for "acquired" here echoes God's own language of "getting" in Genesis 4:1, exposing how the quest for control over life and legacy always slips through human fingers.

1:7.rendering: I piled up silver and gold for myself, the treasure of kings and provinces. I got myself male and female singers, and the delights of the sons of man: many concubines.
1:7.note: The Hebrew word for "delights" echoes the pleasure garden of Eden, but here it twists into a self-made paradise of excess.

1:8.rendering: So I became greater than all who were before me in Jerusalem. And my wisdom remained with me.
1:8.note: The Hebrew word for "greater" echoes the serpent's promise in Genesis 3:5 to be "like God," exposing the futility of Solomon-like wisdom pursued apart from fear of the Lord.

1:9.rendering: I denied my eyes nothing they desired. I refused my heart no pleasure. My heart took delight in all my toil, and this was my reward for all my toil.
1:9.note: The Hebrew word for "delight" here echoes the repeated "toil" language, showing how the Preacher chased joy through endless work only to find it fleeting.

1:10.rendering: Then I looked at all the works my hands had done and at the toil I had labored to achieve, and look, everything was vapour, a chasing after wind. There was no lasting profit under the sun.
1:10.note: The Hebrew word hevel, meaning vapour or breath, appears here like a puff of smoke that vanishes, echoing the book's opening cry in 1:2.

1:11.rendering: Then I turned to see wisdom and madness and folly, for what can the man do who comes after the king? Only what has already been done.
1:11.note: The Hebrew word for "turned" echoes the book's theme of endless cycles, hinting that even the quest for wisdom loops back on itself.

1:12.rendering: I saw that wisdom excels folly as light excels darkness.
1:12.note: The Hebrew wordplay between "light" and "eyes" (both from the same root) hints that wisdom is what truly opens our eyes in a dark world.

1:13.rendering: The wise person has eyes in his head, but the fool walks in darkness. Yet I also saw that one fate happens to them all.
1:13.note: The Hebrew word for "eyes in his head" plays on the idea of sight versus blindness, yet death levels both paths.

1:14.rendering: Then I said in my heart, "What happens to the fool will happen to me also. Why then have I been so exceedingly wise?" And I said in my heart that this also is vapour.
1:14.note: The word "vapour" echoes the book's opening poem, showing even wisdom's edge over folly dissolves like breath.

1:15.rendering: For the wise person dies just like the fool, and so the memory of both will be forgotten in the days to come. How then does the wise die like the fool?
1:15.note: The Hebrew word for "memory" here echoes the name of the teacher himself, Qohelet, hinting that even his own legacy will vanish.

1:16.rendering: So I hated life, because what is done under the sun seemed evil to me. Everything is vapour, a chasing after wind.
1:16.note: The Hebrew word for "hated" echoes God's rejection of evil in the prophets, showing the teacher's disgust mirrors divine displeasure at a broken world.

1:17.rendering: I hated all my toil that I toiled under the sun, which I am leaving to the one who comes after me.
1:17.note: The Hebrew word for "hated" echoes the same strong emotion used for rejecting idols, revealing how even fruitful work can feel pointless when death steals the legacy.

1:18.rendering: And who knows whether he will be a wise person or a fool? Yet he will rule over all the toil that I toiled at and that I became wise under the sun. This also is vapour.
1:18.note: The Hebrew word for "who knows" echoes the teacher's earlier despair in 3:21 and 6:12, underscoring our inability to control what comes after us.

1:19.rendering: So I turned to let my heart despair over all the toil that I had toiled under the sun.
1:19.note: The Hebrew word for "despair" carries the idea of abandoning hope, a stark pivot after the king's exhaustive search for meaning in work.

1:20.rendering: For there is a man whose toil is with wisdom, knowledge, and skill, yet he must leave his portion to a man who has not toiled for it. This also is vapour and a chasing after wind.
1:20.note: The Hebrew word for "portion" echoes the inheritance language of the Promised Land, exposing how death turns even wise labor into something fleeting.

1:21.rendering: For what does a person have from all his toil and the striving of his heart with which he toils under the sun?
1:21.note: The word for "striving" echoes the "chasing after wind" motif that threads through the book, exposing how our driven labor often leaves us grasping at nothing.

1:22.rendering: For all his days are pain and his work is vexation. Even at night his heart does not rest. This also is vapour.
1:22.note: The Hebrew word "ra'yon" for "vexation" echoes the "chasing after wind" motif, showing how toil leaves the heart restless even in sleep.

1:23.rendering: There is nothing better for a person than to eat and drink and find satisfaction in his toil. This also, I saw, is from the hand of God.
1:23.note: Qoheleth's Hebrew for "find satisfaction" literally means "cause his soul to see good," an ancient way of saying enjoy life with your whole being.

1:24.rendering: For who can eat or enjoy himself apart from him?
1:24.note: The Hebrew wordplay on "eat" and "see good" echoes the garden of Eden, where true enjoyment was meant to flow directly from relationship with the Creator.

1:25.rendering: For to the one who is good in his sight, God gives wisdom and knowledge and joy, but to the sinner he gives the task of gathering and collecting only to hand it over to the one who is good in God's sight. This too is vapour and a chasing after wind.
1:25.note: The word for "good in his sight" echoes God's delight in creation, hinting that true enjoyment is a divine gift rather than a human achievement.

0:0.rendering: The words of Qohelet, son of David, king in Jerusalem.
0:0.note: Qohelet means "the assembler" or "the preacher," a title that echoes the gathering of Israel at Solomon's dedication of the temple.

0:1.rendering: Utter vapour, says the Teacher, utter vapour. Everything is vapour.
0:1.note: The Hebrew word hevel, repeated five times here, means not just "vanity" but literally vapour or breath, the same image used for fleeting human life in Psalm 39 and 144.

0:2.rendering: What does a person gain from all the toil at which he labors under the sun?
0:2.note: The Hebrew word for "gain" (yitron) evokes surplus or lasting profit, yet the verse frames human labor as a question that echoes through the book.

0:3.rendering: A generation goes and a generation comes, but the earth stands forever.
0:3.note: The Hebrew word for "stands" echoes the creation language of Genesis 1, where the land emerges to stay put while human life cycles like breath.

0:4.rendering: The sun rises and the sun sets, then it hurries back to the place where it rises again.
0:4.note: This verse echoes the ancient Near Eastern view of the sun's tireless circuit, a vivid image of endless repetition that sets the tone for Qoheleth's meditation on life's cycles.

0:5.rendering: The wind goes to the south, and circles to the north. It circles and circles on its rounds, and the wind returns on its circuits.
0:5.note: The Hebrew word for "wind" is the same as "spirit" or "breath," creating a wordplay that hints at the restless, unseen forces shaping life under the sun.

0:6.rendering: All the streams flow to the sea, yet the sea is never full. To the place where the streams flow, there they return again.
0:6.note: The Hebrew word for "streams" is the same as "rivers" in Genesis 2:10, quietly echoing the original garden's life-giving waters now caught in this weary cycle.

0:7.rendering: All things are wearisome, more than one can say. The eye is never satisfied with seeing, nor the ear filled with hearing.
0:7.note: The Hebrew word for "wearisome" (yĕgĕʿim) carries the sense of exhausting toil, echoing the cursed ground in Genesis 3:17-19 that now drains human effort.

0:8.rendering: What has been is what will be, and what has been done is what will be done, and there is nothing new under the sun.
0:8.note: This Hebrew phrase plays on the word for "under the sun" to paint life as a repetitive cycle observed from a purely earthly vantage point.

0:9.rendering: Is there anything of which one might say, "See this, it is new"? It has already been, in the ages before us.
0:9.note: The Teacher's rhetorical question exposes our illusion of originality, echoing how every generation thinks its discoveries are unprecedented.

0:10.rendering: There is no remembrance of the people who came before us, and of those who will come after us, there will be no remembrance by those who come after them.
0:10.note: The Hebrew word for "remembrance" echoes the urgent refrain of Genesis, where names and stories are meant to endure, yet here the Teacher watches them dissolve like vapour.

0:11.rendering: I, the Teacher, was king over Israel in Jerusalem.
0:11.note: The Hebrew word for "Teacher" is Qoheleth, a title that echoes the gathered assembly or congregation, hinting this is wisdom spoken for the community.

0:12.rendering: I gave my heart to seek and to explore by wisdom everything that is done under heaven. It is a miserable business that God has given to the children of man to be busy with.
0:12.note: The phrase "miserable business" renders the Hebrew "evil task," an ancient Near Eastern expression for the wearisome labor of searching that God has assigned humanity.

0:13.rendering: I have seen all the deeds that are done under the sun, and look, all of them are vapour, and a chasing after wind.
0:13.note: The Hebrew word hevel, often translated "vanity," literally means "vapour" or "breath," evoking something fleeting and ungraspable.

0:14.rendering: What is crooked cannot be straightened, and what is lacking cannot be counted.
0:14.note: The Hebrew wordplay on "straightened" and "lacking" echoes the futility of human toil in a broken creation, inviting slow meditation on limits we cannot fix.

0:15.rendering: I spoke with my own heart, saying, "Look, I have become great and increased in wisdom beyond all who were before me over Jerusalem, and my heart has seen a wealth of wisdom and knowledge."
0:15.note: The Hebrew word for "heart" here carries the ancient sense of the seat of intellect and will, showing the Preacher turning inward to test wisdom's limits.

0:16.rendering: I gave my mind to know wisdom and knowledge, madness and folly. I understood that this too is a chasing after wind.
0:16.note: The Hebrew pairs "wisdom and knowledge" with "madness and folly" in a deliberate echo of the book's opening obsession with opposites, showing even the quest for insight can become futile grasping.

0:17.rendering: For with great wisdom comes great frustration, and the more knowledge, the more sorrow.
0:17.note: The Hebrew wordplay links wisdom (hokmah) directly to vexation (ka'as), showing that deeper insight into life's futility only intensifies grief.

2:0.rendering: For everything there is a season, and a time for every matter under heaven.
2:0.note: The Hebrew word for "season" (zeman) echoes the appointed times of Israel's festivals, reminding us that even life's ordinary rhythms belong to God's ordering.

2:1.rendering: A time to be born, and a time to die. A time to plant, and a time to uproot what is planted.
2:1.note: The Hebrew repeats "time" like a steady drumbeat, inviting us to meditate on the ordered seasons of life under the sun rather than rage against them.

2:2.rendering: a time to kill, and a time to heal; a time to tear down, and a time to build up;
2:2.note: The Hebrew pairs opposites in rhythmic balance, showing how the Teacher sees every human action as having its God-appointed season under the sun.

2:3.rendering: A time to weep, and a time to laugh, a time to mourn, and a time to dance.
2:3.note: The four verbs form two pairs of opposites, echoing the poem's rhythm of human emotion under heaven's seasons.

2:4.rendering: A time to throw stones away, and a time to gather stones together; a time to embrace, and a time to refrain from embracing.
2:4.note: The Hebrew pairs of actions echo ancient Near Eastern treaty language, where scattering and gathering stones could signal war or peace.

2:5.rendering: A time to seek, and a time to lose; a time to keep, and a time to throw away.
2:5.note: The Hebrew word for "lose" (le'abed) echoes the serpent's curse in Genesis 3, where humanity begins the long labor of losing what it seeks to keep.

2:6.rendering: A time to tear and a time to mend, a time to keep silent and a time to speak.
2:6.note: The Hebrew pairs "tear" with "sew" and "silence" with "speak," echoing the ancient practice of ripping garments in grief and repairing them in hope.

2:7.rendering: a time to love and a time to hate, a time for war and a time for peace.
2:7.note: The Hebrew pairs these opposites with the same rhythmic structure as the rest of the poem, showing even enmity and conflict belong inside God's ordered seasons.

2:8.rendering: What gain does the worker have from all his toil?
2:8.note: The word for "gain" here echoes the merchant's profit in Proverbs, but Qohelet keeps finding zero return on life's hardest labor.

2:9.rendering: I have seen the task that God has given to the sons of man to be occupied with.
2:9.note: The word "task" echoes the heavy labor God assigned Adam after Eden, framing human toil as a divine gift meant to shape us.

2:10.rendering: He has made everything beautiful in its time. He has also put eternity into the human heart, yet so that no one can find out the work that God does from beginning to end.
2:10.note: The word for "eternity" is olam, which carries the sense of hidden duration, so God plants a longing for what lies beyond our grasp.

2:11.rendering: I know that there is nothing better for them than to be glad and to do good in their lives.
2:11.note: The ancient sage anchors fleeting joy in simple, repeatable action, not endless striving.

2:12.rendering: and also that everyone should eat and drink and find satisfaction in all their toil, this is God's gift.
2:12.note: The phrase "God's gift" echoes the repeated refrain of enjoyment as a divine endowment, inviting us to receive simple pleasures with open hands rather than grasping for control.

2:13.rendering: I know that everything God does will last forever. Nothing can be added to it, and nothing can be taken from it. God has made it so that people will fear him.
2:13.note: The Hebrew verb for "last forever" echoes the creation command to "be fruitful and multiply," showing God's works as an unchangeable, ongoing project that evokes awe.

2:14.rendering: What has been is now, and what is to be has already been, and God seeks out what is pursued.
2:14.note: The Hebrew verb for "seeks out" carries the sense of God pursuing and summoning even the past, pulling every fleeting moment back into his presence.

2:15.rendering: I saw under the sun that in the place of justice there was wickedness, and in the place of righteousness there was wickedness.
2:15.note: The repeated "place of" wordplay highlights how even the seats of power meant for justice have been overtaken by evil.

2:16.rendering: I said in my heart, God will judge the righteous and the wicked, for there is a time for every purpose and every work.
2:16.note: The word for "purpose" here echoes the "season" of verse 1, hinting that even apparent chaos has its appointed moment under divine scrutiny.

2:17.rendering: I said in my heart concerning the sons of man, that God is testing them and that they might see they are but beasts themselves.
2:17.note: The Hebrew word for "beast" (behemah) deliberately echoes the creation account, reminding us humans share the same breath and fate as animals.

2:18.rendering: For the fate of the sons of man and the fate of the beasts is the same fate: as one dies, so dies the other, and they all have one breath, and the advantage of man over the beast is nothing, for all is vapour.
2:18.note: The Hebrew word for breath, ruach, is the same life-giving wind given to humanity in Genesis 2 and to animals in Genesis 1, underscoring our shared creatureliness.

2:19.rendering: All go to one place. All are from the dust, and all return to dust again.
2:19.note: The word "dust" echoes Genesis 3:19, where humanity's return to the ground after the fall is pronounced.

2:20.rendering: Who knows whether the human spirit rises upward and the animal spirit goes down into the earth?
2:20.note: The rhetorical question echoes the skepticism of Genesis 2-3, where humans and animals both return to dust, challenging easy assumptions about what separates us.

2:21.rendering: So I saw that there is nothing better than for a person to rejoice in his work, because that is his portion. For who can bring him to see what will be after him?
2:21.note: The word "portion" echoes the inheritance language of the Promised Land, turning ordinary toil into the only lasting inheritance we get under the sun.

5:0.rendering: There is an evil that I have seen under the sun, and it weighs heavy on humanity.
5:0.note: The Hebrew word for "evil" here carries the sense of something grievous or oppressive, not just moral wrong, echoing the teacher's earlier laments in chapters 4 and 5.

5:1.rendering: A man to whom God gives riches, wealth, and honor so that he lacks nothing his heart desires, yet God does not give him power to enjoy them, but a stranger eats them instead. This is vapour, and a grievous sickness.
5:1.note: The wordplay on "enjoy" (literally "eat") turns the stranger's literal feasting into a bitter echo of the man's wasted abundance.

5:2.rendering: If a man fathers a hundred children and lives many years, so that his days are many, but his soul is not satisfied with good, and he also has no burial, I say that a miscarriage is better off than he.
5:2.note: The word for "soul" here is nefesh, the throat or appetite, exposing how even a long fruitful life can choke on endless dissatisfaction.

5:3.rendering: For he comes into emptiness and he goes back into darkness, and his name is covered over with darkness.
5:3.note: The Hebrew word for "emptiness" here is hebel, the book's signature term for vapour or breath, echoing how every human life, no matter its length, slips away like mist.

5:4.rendering: Even though it never saw the sun or knew anything, it has more rest than he does.
5:4.note: The stillborn child finds rest in the grave while the wealthy miser finds none, a stark ancient contrast between nonexistence and restless life.

5:5.rendering: Even if he lives two thousand years twice over, yet enjoys no good, do not all go to the one place?
5:5.note: The doubled "two thousand years" echoes the vast lifespans of Genesis patriarchs, yet Qoheleth insists even that longevity collapses into the same death that swallows everyone.

5:6.rendering: All of a person's toil is for their mouth, yet their appetite is never satisfied.
5:6.note: The Hebrew word for "appetite" (nefesh) also means soul, hinting that endless work fails to fill our deepest hunger.

5:7.rendering: For what advantage has the wise person over the fool, or what advantage has the poor person who knows how to walk before the living?
5:7.note: The word for "advantage" echoes the book's opening question about what is truly profitable under the sun.

4:0.rendering: Guard your steps when you go to the house of God. To draw near to listen is better than to offer the sacrifice of fools, for they do not know that they are doing evil.
4:0.note: The Hebrew word for "guard" echoes the same command given at Sinai to keep distance from the mountain, turning worship into a place that still demands holy caution.

4:1.rendering: Do not be hasty with your mouth, and do not let your heart rush to bring a word before God. For God is in heaven and you are on the earth, therefore let your words be few.
4:1.note: The word for "hasty" echoes the snake's rush in Genesis 3, urging measured speech before the God who is utterly above us.

4:2.rendering: For a dream comes with many worries, and the voice of a fool with many words.
4:2.note: The Hebrew pairs "dream" with "anxiety" and "fool" with "many words," exposing how restless busyness often masks emptiness.

4:3.rendering: When you make a vow to God, do not delay to fulfill it. He takes no pleasure in fools. Fulfill the vow you have made.
4:3.note: The Hebrew word for vow echoes the same root used for Jacob's urgent promise at Bethel, turning a personal commitment into a mirror for how seriously God treats our words.

4:4.rendering: It is better that you should not vow than that you should vow and not pay.
4:4.note: The Teacher echoes Deuteronomy's warnings about vows, exposing how our words can trap us in the ancient world of binding oaths.

4:5.rendering: Do not let your mouth bring guilt on your flesh, and do not say before the messenger that it was a mistake. Why should God be angry at your voice and destroy the work of your hands?
4:5.note: The Hebrew word for "messenger" here is the same as "angel," hinting that your rash vow reaches God's heavenly court.

4:6.rendering: For in the multitude of dreams and many words there is emptiness. Rather, fear God.
4:6.note: The Hebrew word for "emptiness" here is the same hevel that opens the book, linking vain chatter and wishful dreams to the book's core theme of fleeting vapour.

4:7.rendering: If you see the poor oppressed in a province, and justice and right taken away, do not be shocked at the matter, for one official watches over another, and higher ones over them.
4:7.note: The verse exposes a pyramid of bureaucratic oppression in the ancient world, where each layer exploits the one below.

3:0.rendering: I saw all the oppressions that are done under the sun, and look, the tears of the oppressed, with no one to comfort them. Power was on the side of their oppressors, and they had no one to comfort them.
3:0.note: The repeated phrase "no one to comfort them" echoes the lament language of Lamentations, turning a wisdom observation into a communal cry of pain.

3:1.rendering: So I praised the dead, who have already died, more than the living, who are still alive.
3:1.note: Qohelet turns ancient honor for the living on its head, echoing Job's longing for the grave as refuge from endless toil.

3:2.rendering: But better than both is the one who has not yet been, who has not seen the evil deeds that are done under the sun.
3:2.note: This shocking escalation names the unborn as luckiest, exposing how thoroughly the Preacher sees oppression and toil as woven into the fabric of life under the sun.

3:3.rendering: I saw that all toil and all skill in work spring from one person's envy of another. This too is vapour, a chasing after wind.
3:3.note: The Hebrew word for "envy" here carries the double edge of zeal and jealousy, revealing how even our best work can be poisoned by comparison.

3:4.rendering: The fool folds his hands and eats his own flesh.
3:4.note: This proverb plays on the image of a lazy man whose idle hands lead to self-destruction, echoing the Hebrew wordplay between "folds" and consuming himself in poverty.

3:5.rendering: Better is a handful of quietness than two fists full of toil and chasing after wind.
3:5.note: The Hebrew wordplay on "handful" versus "two fists" paints a picture of grasping versus resting, echoing the book's wisdom that frantic accumulation is just wind-chasing.

3:6.rendering: Again I saw something pointless under the sun: there was one person without a second, he had neither son nor brother, yet there was no end to all his toil, and his eye was never satisfied with riches.
3:6.note: The Hebrew word for "pointless" (hebel) echoes the book's opening breath-like vapour, exposing how solitary striving devours life without anyone to share the fruit.

3:7.rendering: There is one who is alone, with no second, and he has neither son nor brother. Yet there is no end to all his toil, and his eyes are not satisfied with riches. For whom am I toiling, he asks, and depriving my soul of good? This also is vapour, a chasing after wind.
3:7.note: The Hebrew word for alone echoes the creation cry in Genesis 2 that it is not good for the human to be alone, yet this solitary striver chooses isolation for more stuff.

5:8.rendering: Better is the sight of the eyes than the wandering of the soul. This too is vapour, and a chasing after wind.
5:8.note: The Hebrew pits "seeing with the eyes" against the soul's restless roaming, a wisdom jab at endless desire that echoes the book's opening chase after wind.

5:9.rendering: Whatever exists has already been named, and what a human being is has been known. He cannot contend with the one who is stronger than he.
5:9.note: The Hebrew word for "named" echoes Genesis 2, where God names humanity, reminding us our limits were set from the very beginning.

5:10.rendering: The more words there are, the more emptiness increases. What profit does that bring a person?
5:10.note: The Hebrew word for "words" (debarim) also means "things," so this line plays on how both talk and possessions multiply futility.

5:11.rendering: For who knows what is good for a person in life, during the few days of his fleeting life that pass like a shadow? Who can tell a person what will happen under the sun after him?
5:11.note: The Hebrew word for "shadow" echoes the fleeting breath of Genesis 1, underscoring how human plans dissolve before the Creator's hidden purposes.

4:8.rendering: A double yield comes from the land: the king himself is served by the field.
4:8.note: The Hebrew word for "profit" echoes throughout the book, yet here the land itself quietly yields more than human schemes.

4:9.rendering: Whoever loves silver will never have enough silver, and whoever loves wealth will never have enough income. This too is vapour.
4:9.note: The word for "wealth" here echoes the abundance God promises in the Torah, yet the Teacher shows how chasing it leads only to vapour.

4:10.rendering: As goods increase, so do those who consume them. What advantage then does the owner have, except to watch them with his own eyes?
4:10.note: The Hebrew word for "increase" echoes the creation mandate to multiply, but here it twists into futility, as more only means more mouths to feed.

4:11.rendering: The sleep of the worker is sweet, whether he eats little or much, but the abundance of the rich will not let him sleep.
4:11.note: This proverb plays on the Hebrew word for "sweet" sleep, contrasting the simple laborer's rest with the rich man's insomnia caused by worry over his overflowing possessions.

4:12.rendering: There is a sickening evil I have seen under the sun: riches hoarded by their owner to his own harm.
4:12.note: The Hebrew word for "sickening" (chalah) echoes the pain of childbirth, turning hoarded wealth into a grotesque self-wound.

4:13.rendering: Those riches are lost through a bad venture. When he fathers a son, there is nothing in his hand to give him.
4:13.note: The Hebrew word for "bad venture" (ra'ah) echoes the "evil" that Qohelet sees under the sun, turning wealth into vapour that vanishes like breath.

4:14.rendering: As he came out from his mother's womb, naked he will return, going as he came. He gains no profit from his toil that he can carry in his hand.
4:14.note: The Hebrew word for "profit" here echoes the book's opening question, "What does a person gain?"

4:15.rendering: This also is a sickening evil: exactly as he came, so shall he go, and what profit does he have who toils for the wind?
4:15.note: The Hebrew word for "sickening" (choli) echoes the "vanity" of 1:2, painting toil as a nauseating wind-chase with nothing to show.

4:16.rendering: All his days he eats in darkness, with much vexation and sickness and resentment.
4:16.note: The Hebrew word for "vexation" echoes the "striving after wind" that haunts the book, painting a portrait of joyless existence under the sun.

4:17.rendering: Look, what I have seen to be good and fitting is this: to eat and to drink and to find satisfaction in all one's toil under the sun during the few days of life that God has given him, for this is his lot.
4:17.note: The Hebrew word for "lot" here echoes the dividing of the promised land, suggesting that joyful work is the inheritance God allots each person under the sun.

4:18.rendering: Moreover, for every person to whom God has given riches and wealth, and has given him the power to eat from it and to take his portion and to rejoice in his toil, this is a gift of God.
4:18.note: The phrase "gift of God" echoes the same Hebrew word used for the "portion" in Ecclesiastes 3:22 and 9:9, showing joy in daily work as one of the few reliable anchors in a vapour-filled life.

4:19.rendering: For he will not much remember the days of his life, because God keeps him occupied with the joy of his heart.
4:19.note: The Hebrew word for "keeps him occupied" carries the idea of God answering or responding to the person's heart, turning fleeting days into absorbed joy.

3:8.rendering: Two are better than one, because they have a good reward for their toil.
3:8.note: The Hebrew word for "two" (shenayim) echoes the creation pattern of paired companions in Genesis, where solitude is declared "not good."

3:9.rendering: For if they fall, one will lift up his companion. But woe to the one who falls alone, for there is no one to lift him up.
3:9.note: The Hebrew word for "companion" here echoes the creation mandate for a fitting helper, showing how ancient wisdom values mutual rescue over rugged independence.

3:10.rendering: Also, if two lie down together, they keep warm. But how can one person keep warm alone?
3:10.note: The Hebrew verb for "keep warm" echoes the same word used for a man "clinging" to his wife in Genesis 2:24, quietly linking solitary toil back to the original design for human companionship.

3:11.rendering: Though one person can be overpowered, two can stand back to back and defend themselves. A three-stranded cord cannot be torn apart quickly.
3:11.note: The Hebrew word for "cord" here echoes the creation language of "twining" in Genesis, suggesting that human community participates in the Creator's own work of binding strength together.

3:12.rendering: Better is a poor but wise youth than an old and foolish king who no longer knows how to take advice.
3:12.note: The Hebrew word for "youth" here echoes the same term used for young Joseph and young David, hinting that true wisdom often emerges from the overlooked rather than the established.

3:13.rendering: For he came out of prison to become king, even though he was born poor in his kingdom.
3:13.note: This proverb spotlights a shocking reversal: a nobody from prison outrises a foolish king, echoing Joseph's rags-to-riches rise in Genesis.

3:14.rendering: I saw all the living who walk under the sun with the youth who rises up in place of the old king.
3:14.note: The Hebrew word for "youth" echoes the same term used for the vulnerable young successor in 4:13, hinting that even fresh power will one day grow old and be replaced.

3:15.rendering: There was no end to all the people before him. Yet those who come later will not rejoice in him. This too is vapour, and a chasing after wind.
3:15.note: The word "before" (Hebrew lipnei) creates a wordplay on both "in front of" and "in the presence of," highlighting how quickly a leader's popularity fades.

6:0.rendering: A good name is better than precious ointment, and the day of death is better than the day of birth.
6:0.note: The Hebrew wordplay links "name" (shem) with "oil" (shemen), suggesting reputation outlasts even the finest perfumes used in both celebration and burial.

6:1.rendering: It is better to go to the house of mourning than to go to the house of feasting, for this is the end of all mankind, and the living will lay it to heart.
6:1.note: The Hebrew pairs "house of mourning" and "house of feasting" to jolt us: death's house teaches what life's parties never can.

6:2.rendering: Sorrow is better than laughter, for by the sadness of the face the heart is made glad.
6:2.note: The Hebrew wordplay links "sadness of face" (ra'ah panim) to the "goodness" (tov) of a heart that has been shaped by grief, echoing how ancient wisdom saw mourning as the path to deeper joy.

6:3.rendering: The heart of the wise is in the house of mourning, but the heart of fools is in the house of gladness.
6:3.note: The Hebrew word for "heart" here echoes the book's earlier call to guard the inner self, revealing wisdom as the choice to face mortality over endless distraction.

6:4.rendering: It is better to hear the rebuke of a wise person than to listen to the song of fools.
6:4.note: The Hebrew word for "rebuke" here is the same one used in Proverbs for the sharp correction that shapes character, contrasting the empty flattery hidden in fools' songs.

6:5.rendering: For like the crackling of thorns under a pot, so is the laughter of a fool. This too is vapour.
6:5.note: The Hebrew wordplay on qol (voice/sound) links the fool's loud laughter to the useless snapping of thorns that flare up and die quickly.

6:6.rendering: Surely oppression makes the wise into fools, and a bribe destroys the heart.
6:6.note: The Hebrew word for "oppression" here echoes the exact term used for the ruthless taskmasters in Egypt, showing how power that crushes others also crushes the one who wields it.

6:7.rendering: Better is the end of a matter than its beginning. Better a patient spirit than a haughty one.
6:7.note: The Hebrew word for "end" echoes the book's opening "vanity" refrain, suggesting that only by enduring to the conclusion can we discern any meaning at all.

7:0.rendering: Who is like the wise person? Who knows the meaning of a matter? A person's wisdom brightens their face and changes the harshness of their countenance.
7:0.note: The Hebrew word for "interpretation" here is the same one used for unraveling dreams and riddles in the Joseph story, hinting that true wisdom deciphers life's puzzles.

7:1.rendering: I say: Keep the king's command, because of your oath to God.
7:1.note: The Hebrew word for "oath" (shevuah) echoes the covenant oaths that bind Israel to Yahweh, subtly framing even a pagan king's authority as under divine order.

7:2.rendering: Do not be hasty to go from his presence. Do not persist in an evil matter, for he does whatever he pleases.
7:2.note: The Hebrew word for "hasty" echoes the "time for every matter" in chapter 3, urging measured wisdom before a powerful king who answers to no one.

7:3.rendering: For the word of the king is powerful, and who can say to him, "What are you doing?"
7:3.note: The Hebrew "word" here doubles as both command and decree, echoing the king's absolute authority in the ancient Near East.

7:4.rendering: Whoever keeps the command will not experience harm, and a wise heart knows the right time and procedure.
7:4.note: The Hebrew links "time" and "procedure" in a single phrase, echoing the ordered seasons of Ecclesiastes 3.

7:5.rendering: For every matter has its time and its proper way, yet the evil of man weighs heavily on him.
7:5.note: The Hebrew word for "evil" here also means "trouble" or "misfortune," showing how human calamity presses down like an unbearable load even when we know the right season.

7:6.rendering: For no one knows what is going to happen. So who can tell him when it will happen?
7:6.note: The Hebrew word for "know" here echoes the same root in Genesis 3, where humanity reached for godlike knowledge of good and evil, yet remained blind to the future.

7:7.rendering: No one has power over the wind to restrain the wind, and no one has power over the day of death. There is no release during war, and wickedness will not deliver those who practice it.
7:7.note: The Hebrew word for "wind" is the same as "spirit" or "breath," creating a wordplay that ties human limits over life itself to our inability to control the spirit.

7:8.rendering: All this I saw, as I applied my heart to every deed that is done under the sun, when one person has power over another to his own harm.
7:8.note: The phrase "one person has power over another" echoes the earlier refrain in 4:1 about the tears of the oppressed with no comforter, showing how unchecked authority inevitably turns to harm.

7:9.rendering: Then I saw the wicked buried, and they came and went from the holy place, and they were praised in the city where they had acted so. This also is vapour.
7:9.note: The Hebrew word for "praise" here ironically echoes the language of temple worship, turning public honor for the wicked into another fleeting breath.

7:10.rendering: Because the sentence for an evil deed is not carried out quickly, the hearts of people are fully set on doing evil.
7:10.note: The word for "sentence" here echoes the legal decrees of ancient kings, revealing how delayed justice breeds bolder rebellion.

7:11.rendering: Though a sinner does evil a hundred times and prolongs his days, yet I know that it will be well for those who fear God, who fear before him.
7:11.note: The Preacher wrestles with delayed justice, a tension echoed in Psalm 73 and Habakkuk 1, yet anchors hope in the ancient wisdom that fearing God ultimately shapes a life that endures.

7:12.rendering: But it will not go well for the wicked person, and his days will not lengthen like a shadow, because he does not fear God.
7:12.note: The Hebrew wordplay on "lengthen" echoes the shadow that stretches long in the sun yet vanishes, showing how the wicked's life shrinks to nothing despite appearances.

7:13.rendering: There is a vapour that happens on the earth: there are righteous people who get what the wicked deserve, and there are wicked people who get what the righteous deserve. I say that this too is vapour.
7:13.note: The word "vapour" echoes the book's opening verdict on all of life under the sun, framing even this injustice as part of the fleeting mystery we cannot fully untangle.

7:14.rendering: So I praised enjoyment, because there is nothing better for a person under the sun than to eat and drink and enjoy himself. This will accompany him in his toil through the days of his life that God has given him under the sun.
7:14.note: The Hebrew word for enjoyment here echoes the same term for "pleasure" that the Preacher tested and found empty in chapter 2, showing how wisdom reframes fleeting gifts as companionship in toil.

7:15.rendering: When I applied my heart to know wisdom, and to see the business that is done on the earth, for even by day and by night one sees no sleep with his eyes,
7:15.note: The phrase "sees no sleep with his eyes" captures the Hebrew idiom for obsessive, sleepless toil, echoing the Preacher's earlier experiments in 1:13 and 2:23.

6:8.rendering: Do not be quick in your spirit to become angry, for anger lodges in the lap of fools.
6:8.note: The Hebrew word for "anger" here echoes the "vexation" of 1:18 and 2:23, showing how this hot emotion turns even the wise into fools by taking up permanent residence.

6:9.rendering: Do not say, "Why were the former days better than these?" For it is not from wisdom that you ask this.
6:9.note: The Hebrew word for "better" echoes the creation refrain in Genesis, exposing how nostalgia often masks a lack of wisdom.

6:10.rendering: Wisdom is good along with an inheritance, and an advantage to those who see the sun.
6:10.note: The Hebrew word for "good" here echoes the creation refrain in Genesis, suggesting wisdom as a fresh creative gift under the sun.

6:11.rendering: For wisdom is a shelter just as money is a shelter, but the advantage of knowledge is this: wisdom preserves the life of the one who has it.
6:11.note: The Hebrew word for "shelter" (tsel) pictures both wisdom and money as shade from the desert sun, yet only wisdom truly keeps its owner alive.

6:12.rendering: Consider the work of God. Who can straighten what he has made crooked?
6:12.note: The Hebrew word for "crooked" (me'uvath) is the same one used in Ecclesiastes 1:15 for what cannot be fixed, echoing the book's opening poem on life's unchangeable vanities.

6:13.rendering: In the day of prosperity, be joyful. And in the day of adversity, consider this: God has made the one as well as the other, so that a person cannot discover anything that will come after him.
6:13.note: The word for "consider" echoes the book's call to wise observation, inviting us to see God's hand behind both good and bad without claiming to predict the future.

6:14.rendering: In my days of futility I have seen it all: a righteous person perishing in their righteousness, and a wicked person living long in their evil.
6:14.note: The word for "futility" (hevel) echoes the book's opening, framing this as one more vapour-like contradiction observed under the sun.

6:15.rendering: Do not be overly righteous, and do not act too wise. Why should you destroy yourself?
6:15.note: The Hebrew word for "destroy yourself" carries the sense of being stunned or made desolate, a warning against the self-destructive extremes of rigid piety in a broken world.

6:16.rendering: Do not be excessively wicked, and do not be a fool. Why should you die before your time?
6:16.note: The Hebrew word for "fool" here carries the idea of moral stupidity that invites self-destruction, echoing the wisdom tradition's warning that certain paths lead to an early grave.

6:17.rendering: It is good that you grasp one thing without letting go of the other, for the one who fears God will come out from them all.
6:17.note: The Hebrew word for "grasp" echoes the same verb used for holding onto wisdom in Proverbs, urging balanced reverence over rigid extremes.

6:18.rendering: Wisdom gives more strength to the wise than ten rulers who are in the city.
6:18.note: The Hebrew word for "strength" here is the same one used for the fortified walls of a city, turning wisdom into an inner citadel more reliable than any outer defense.

6:19.rendering: Surely there is not a righteous person on earth who does good and never sins.
6:19.note: The Hebrew word for "righteous" here echoes the same term used for Noah and Job, yet even they stumbled, underscoring the universal human tilt toward failure.

6:20.rendering: Do not take to heart all the words that people speak, lest you hear your servant cursing you.
6:20.note: The Hebrew verb for "take to heart" echoes the same phrase used for heeding wisdom in Proverbs, warning that obsessive attention to every insult will unravel even a wise person's peace.

6:21.rendering: For you also know, in your own heart, how many times you yourself have cursed others.
6:21.note: The Hebrew "also" (gam) links your heart's secret curses to the very same human folly you just observed in others.

6:22.rendering: All this I tested with wisdom. I said, "I will be wise," but it was far from me.
6:22.note: The Hebrew word for "far" echoes the same term used for the inaccessibility of God in 3:11 and 8:17, showing wisdom's elusiveness even to the seeker.

6:23.rendering: Whatever has been is far off and very deep, who can find it out?
6:23.note: The Hebrew word for "deep" here echoes the primordial abyss of Genesis 1:2, hinting that the quest for wisdom keeps bumping into the same unsearchable mystery that only God can navigate.

7:16.rendering: Then I saw all the work of God, that a person cannot discover the work that is done under the sun. Though a person may toil in seeking, they will not find it out. Even if the wise person claims to know, they cannot find it out.
7:16.note: The Hebrew word for "discover" echoes the same root as "search out" in 7:24, tying this frustration to the quest for wisdom itself.

6:24.rendering: I turned my heart to know, to explore, and to seek out wisdom and the scheme of things, and to know that wickedness is folly and foolishness is madness.
6:24.note: The Hebrew word for "scheme" here is the same one used for the inscrutable "work" of God in Ecclesiastes 3:11, hinting that the quest itself may be chasing after wind.

6:25.rendering: I found something more bitter than death: the woman who is a snare, whose heart is nets, whose hands are chains. The one who pleases God will escape her, but the sinner will be captured by her.
6:25.note: The Hebrew wordplay on "nets" and "snares" echoes the traps laid for animals in ancient wisdom literature.

6:26.rendering: "Look," says the Teacher, "this is what I have found," says the Teacher, "adding one to one to find the sum."
6:26.note: The word for "sum" or "account" here is the same Hebrew root used for the book's title, Qoheleth, hinting that the search itself is what we're meant to ponder.

6:27.rendering: I have searched and searched and found one man among a thousand, but I have not found a woman among all these.
6:27.note: The Hebrew verb for "search" echoes the king's desperate quest in chapter 2, exposing the limits of wisdom pursued apart from the fear of God.

6:28.rendering: Look, this alone I have found: God made humanity upright, but they have sought out many schemes.
6:28.note: The word for "schemes" echoes the serpent's cunning in Genesis 3, revealing how our bent toward calculation fractures the original straightness of Eden.

10:0.rendering: Send your bread upon the face of the waters, for in many days you will find it again.
10:0.note: The Hebrew word for "send" carries the sense of casting or throwing, like a farmer sowing seed on unpredictable floodwaters in ancient irrigation cultures.

10:1.rendering: Give a portion to seven, or even to eight, for you do not know what disaster may happen on the earth.
10:1.note: The numbers seven and eight echo a common ancient Near Eastern pattern for completeness plus one more, urging generous scattering of resources against unpredictable calamity.

10:2.rendering: If the clouds are full of rain, they empty themselves on the earth, and if a tree falls to the south or to the north, in the place where the tree falls, there it will lie.
10:2.note: Like rain from heavy clouds, life moves in irreversible directions, once a tree falls its spot is fixed.

10:3.rendering: Whoever watches the wind will never sow, and whoever stares at the clouds will never reap.
10:3.note: The Hebrew word for "watch" here echoes the anxious gaze of the farmer in Genesis 2-3, a wisdom warning against paralysis by overanalyzing uncertain conditions.

10:4.rendering: Just as you do not know the path of the wind or how bones grow together in a mother's womb, so you cannot know the work of God who does all things.
10:4.note: The verse links the mysterious paths of wind and fetal development, two ancient wonders, to remind us how little we grasp of God's hidden work.

10:5.rendering: Sow your seed in the morning, and do not rest your hand in the evening. For you do not know which will succeed, this one or that, or whether both of them alike will be good.
10:5.note: The repeated "do not let your hand rest" echoes the urgent diligence of ancient farmers who hedged their bets against unpredictable weather and soil.

10:6.rendering: Light is sweet, and it is good for the eyes to see the sun.
10:6.note: The Hebrew word for "sweet" here echoes the delight of wisdom literature, reminding us that even in a book full of vanity, the simple gift of seeing daylight still calls for gratitude.

10:7.rendering: Indeed, if a person lives many years, let him rejoice in them all. But let him remember the days of darkness, for they will be many. Everything that is coming is vapour.
10:7.note: The Hebrew word hevel, translated "vapour," echoes throughout Ecclesiastes like a refrain, reminding us that even a long life of joy is fleeting and must be held lightly.

9:0.rendering: Dead flies make the perfumer's oil stink and bubble over, so a little folly outweighs wisdom and honor.
9:0.note: The Hebrew wordplay links "flies" (zebub) with "bubble over" (yabiah), turning a tiny corruption into something that ruins everything.

9:1.rendering: A wise person's heart inclines to the right, but a fool's heart to the left.
9:1.note: In ancient thought the right side signaled skill and favor while the left meant clumsiness, an image that quietly shapes our word "sinister."

9:2.rendering: Even when a fool walks along the road, he lacks sense, and he tells everyone that he is a fool.
9:2.note: The Hebrew word for "lacks sense" literally means his heart is missing, an ancient way of saying he has no inner compass at all.

9:3.rendering: If the ruler's spirit rises against you, do not leave your post, for calmness can undo great offenses.
9:3.note: The Hebrew word for "calmness" echoes the "healing" that quiet words bring in Proverbs, showing how composure disarms rage in the ancient royal court.

9:4.rendering: There is an evil I have seen under the sun, like an error that goes out from before the ruler.
9:4.note: The word for "error" carries the sense of a blunder or slip-up, echoing the king's own foolishness described in the verses right before it.

9:5.rendering: Folly is set in many high positions, while the rich sit in a low place.
9:5.note: The Hebrew word for "folly" (sakal) creates a bitter wordplay with the idea of a "high" seat meant for the wise, exposing the upside-down world of corrupt courts in the ancient Near East.

9:6.rendering: I have seen slaves on horses, and princes walking on the ground like slaves.
9:6.note: The Hebrew wordplay on "slaves" and "princes" highlights a world turned upside down, echoing the reversals in Proverbs 30:22 and 19:10.

9:7.rendering: Whoever digs a pit will fall into it, and whoever breaks through a wall a snake will bite him.
9:7.note: The Hebrew wordplay links digging a pit (bor) with falling into calamity, echoing the ancient Near Eastern idea that your own schemes can trap you.

8:0.rendering: For all this I laid to heart, even to explore it all: that the righteous and the wise and their deeds are in the hand of God, whether it is love or hate, man does not know, all lies before them.
8:0.note: The phrase "in the hand of God" echoes the ancient image of a potter shaping clay, yet here even the outcomes of love and hate remain hidden from us.

8:1.rendering: Everything turns out the same for everyone: one fate comes to the righteous and the wicked, to the good and the clean and the unclean, to the one who sacrifices and the one who does not sacrifice. As it is for the good, so it is for the sinner; as it is for the one who swears an oath, so it is for the one who fears swearing an oath.
8:1.note: The word "fate" here is the same Hebrew term for "chance" or "event" used in the story of the wise woman of Tekoa, reminding us that death levels all human distinctions like a great equalizer.

8:2.rendering: This is the evil in everything that happens under the sun: the same fate comes to all. The hearts of humans are full of evil, and madness is in their hearts while they live, and after that they go to the dead.
8:2.note: The Hebrew word for "fate" (miqreh) echoes the "happening" of chapter 3's seasons, showing how death levels every human story under the sun.

8:3.rendering: Whoever is joined to all the living has hope, for a living dog is better than a dead lion.
8:3.note: The Hebrew wordplay on "joined" (chabar) and "hope" (bitachon) hints that clinging to life itself is the only real tether to possibility.

8:4.rendering: The living know that they will die, but the dead know nothing at all. They have no more reward, and even the memory of them is forgotten.
8:4.note: This stark observation about death echoes the ancient Near Eastern view of Sheol as a place of shadowy non-existence, inviting meditation on what truly lasts.

8:5.rendering: Their love and their hate and their envy have already perished, and they no longer have any share forever in anything that is done under the sun.
8:5.note: The triple repetition of emotions, love, hate, envy, echoes the earlier poem on times and seasons, showing how death freezes all human striving into silence.

8:6.rendering: Go, eat your bread with joy and drink your wine with a glad heart, for God has already approved what you do.
8:6.note: The Hebrew verbs here echo the language of temple sacrifices, turning everyday meals into acts of glad acceptance before God.

8:7.rendering: Let your clothes always be white, and let oil never be lacking on your head.
8:7.note: In ancient Near Eastern culture, white garments and fresh oil signaled joy and readiness for feasting, not mourning.

10:8.rendering: Rejoice, young man, in your youth, and let your heart cheer you in the days of your prime. Walk in the ways of your heart and in the sight of your eyes, but know that for all these things God will bring you into judgment.
10:8.note: The Hebrew verb for "rejoice" echoes the same word used for joy at harvest and wedding feasts, urging full-hearted living while the repeated "know" plants a sobering wisdom hook.

10:9.rendering: So banish anxiety from your heart, and put away pain from your flesh, for childhood and youth are vapour.
10:9.note: The word for "youth" here is yaldut, the same root as "child," echoing the fleeting "vapour" (hebel) that opens the book.

9:8.rendering: Whoever quarries stones will be hurt by them, and whoever splits logs is endangered by them.
9:8.note: The Hebrew wordplay on 'risk' and 'danger' echoes the Preacher's theme that even skilled labor carries unpredictable peril in a broken world.

9:9.rendering: If the iron is blunt and one does not sharpen the edge, then one must exert more strength, but wisdom has the advantage of giving success.
9:9.note: The Hebrew wordplay on "strength" (koach) links blunt tools to foolish effort, showing how wisdom multiplies effectiveness without brute force.

9:10.rendering: If the snake bites before it is charmed, there is no advantage for the charmer.
9:10.note: The Hebrew word for "snake" echoes the serpent in Eden, reminding us that wisdom's timing matters more than skill.

9:11.rendering: The words of a wise person's mouth bring favor, but the lips of a fool swallow him up.
9:11.note: In Hebrew the fool's lips "swallow" him, a vivid image of self-destruction that echoes the serpent's fate in Genesis 3:14.

9:12.rendering: The beginning of his words is folly, and the end of his speech is wicked madness.
9:12.note: The Hebrew word order traces a downward spiral from silly talk to dangerous insanity, exposing how unchecked speech corrupts from within.

9:13.rendering: A fool multiplies words. No one knows what will happen, and who can tell him what will be after him?
9:13.note: The Hebrew piles up the verb for "multiply" right next to "words," echoing the Teacher's earlier warning that too many words lead to folly.

9:14.rendering: The fool's toil wears him out, for he does not even know the way to the city.
9:14.note: The Hebrew plays on "amal" (toil/weariness), showing how the fool's frantic labor exhausts him without direction, echoing the book's theme of life under the sun.

9:15.rendering: Woe to you, land whose king is a boy, and whose princes feast in the morning.
9:15.note: The Hebrew word for "boy" here evokes immaturity rather than literal childhood, contrasting sharply with the ideal king who brings order instead of revelry.

8:8.rendering: Enjoy life with the wife you love all the days of your fleeting life that God has given you under the sun, all the days of your vapour, because that is your portion in life and in your toil that you toil under the sun.
8:8.note: The word for "enjoy" here is the same one used for "see good" in the garden of Eden, inviting us to savor ordinary married life as a fleeting gift before the fall's shadow lengthens.

8:9.rendering: Whatever your hand finds to do, do it with all your might, for there is no work or planning or knowledge or wisdom in the grave where you are going.
8:9.note: The Hebrew piles up four active nouns, work, planning, knowledge, wisdom, then slams the door with Sheol, a stark ancient reminder that death ends every human project.

8:10.rendering: I saw again under the sun that the race does not belong to the swift, nor the battle to the strong, nor bread to the wise, nor riches to the intelligent, nor favor to the skillful, but time and chance happen to all of them.
8:10.note: The Hebrew word for "time" here echoes the famous poem in chapter 3, revealing life's outcomes as mysteriously timed rather than earned by merit.

8:11.rendering: For man does not know his time. Like fish that are caught in an evil net, and like birds that are caught in a snare, so the sons of man are ensnared at an evil time, when it suddenly falls upon them.
8:11.note: The word for "time" here echoes the "season" of chapter 3, showing how humans get snared precisely when they think they control their moment.

8:12.rendering: I also saw this example of wisdom under the sun, and it struck me as great.
8:12.note: The Hebrew word for "great" here carries the weight of something weighty and impressive, yet the story that follows reveals how easily such wisdom gets forgotten.

8:13.rendering: There was a small city with few people in it, and a great king came against it and surrounded it and built great siege works against it.
8:13.note: The Hebrew word for "few" (məʿaṭ) echoes the vulnerable state of Israel in other texts, like Deuteronomy, where God chooses the smallest nation to display his power.

8:14.rendering: There was found in it a poor wise man, and he delivered the city by his wisdom, yet no one remembered that poor man.
8:14.note: The Hebrew word for "remembered" carries the weight of active honor, yet the story shows wisdom's quiet power is quickly forgotten.

8:15.rendering: Then I said, "Wisdom is better than strength, yet the poor man's wisdom is despised and his words are not heard."
8:15.note: In Hebrew the word for "despised" echoes the earlier refrain that everything is "vanity," showing even wisdom gets swept into the same breath-like futility when the wise person stays poor.

9:16.rendering: Blessed are you, land, whose king is a son of nobles, and whose princes eat at the proper time, for strength and not for drunkenness.
9:16.note: In the ancient Near East, a noble-born king with self-controlled officials was seen as a rare blessing that prevented the kind of chaos pictured in the previous verse.

9:17.rendering: Through laziness the rafters cave in, and through idle hands the house leaks.
9:17.note: The Hebrew wordplay links slack hands to a sagging house, turning everyday neglect into a vivid picture of how small choices unravel order.

9:18.rendering: A feast is made for laughter, and wine gladdens life, but silver answers everything.
9:18.note: The punchy Hebrew wordplay on "silver answers everything" nods to how money talks in every situation, yet the verse sits in a chapter mocking misplaced priorities.

9:19.rendering: Even in your bedroom do not curse the king, and in your inner rooms do not curse the rich, for a bird of the air will carry the sound, and a winged creature will report the matter.
9:19.note: The Hebrew wordplay on "bird" (oph) and "winged one" (ba'al kanaph) turns gossip into a vivid image of inescapable rumor in ancient court life.

8:16.rendering: The quiet words of the wise are heard better than the shouting of a ruler among fools.
8:16.note: In Hebrew the contrast hinges on "heard" echoing the book's opening call to listen, showing wisdom's subtle power over volume.

8:17.rendering: Wisdom is better than weapons of war, but one sinner destroys much good.
8:17.note: The Hebrew word for "weapons" here echoes the tools of violence in earlier wisdom warnings, showing how one fool's outburst can unravel the careful order wisdom builds.

11:0.rendering: Remember your creator in the days of your youth, before the evil days come and the years draw near when you will say, "I have no pleasure in them."
11:0.note: The Hebrew word for "creator" (bor'ekha) echoes the name of the first human in Genesis (bara), inviting us to re-anchor our fleeting days in the One who shaped us from dust.

11:1.rendering: Before the sun and the light and the moon and the stars grow dark, and the clouds return after the rain.
11:1.note: The verse layers cosmic imagery of fading light over the slow decline of old age, echoing Genesis 1's creation order in reverse.

11:2.rendering: In the day when the keepers of the house tremble, and the strong men stoop, and the grinders cease because they are few, and those who look through the windows grow dim,
11:2.note: The poem's vivid household imagery, drawn from ancient village life, pictures the body's gradual decay in old age: arms shake, legs weaken, teeth fall out, and eyes fail.

11:3.rendering: When the doors on the street are shut, and the sound of the mill grows faint, when one rises at the sound of a bird, and all the daughters of song are brought low.
11:3.note: The shutting doors and fading mill sounds paint the failing senses of old age, with the twittering dawn birds now too loud to sleep through.

11:4.rendering: Also they are afraid of heights, and terrors are on the road. The almond tree blossoms, the grasshopper drags itself along, and desire fails, because man goes to his eternal home, and the mourners go about the streets.
11:4.note: The almond tree blossoms white like an old man's hair, while the grasshopper's slow crawl paints a picture of frail old age, an ancient word-picture of life's winter.

11:5.rendering: Before the silver cord is snapped, or the golden bowl is shattered, or the pitcher is broken at the fountain, or the wheel broken at the cistern,
11:5.note: These four vivid images of broken vessels echo the frailty of life in an ancient world where such everyday objects were essential for light, water, and survival.

11:6.rendering: The dust returns to the earth as it was, and the breath returns to God who gave it.
11:6.note: This verse echoes Genesis 2:7 and 3:19, closing the human life cycle with the spirit returning to its divine source.

11:7.rendering: "Absolute vapour," says the Teacher, "everything is vapour."
11:7.note: The Hebrew hebel, repeated like a refrain, means not just "vanity" but fleeting breath or mist, echoing the book's opening in 1:2.

11:8.rendering: And moreover, the Teacher was wise: he taught the people knowledge, and he weighed, examined, and arranged many proverbs.
11:8.note: The Hebrew word for "arranged" carries the idea of straightening or ordering, showing how wisdom shapes scattered observations into something enduring.

11:9.rendering: The Teacher searched to find just the right words, and what he wrote was upright and true.
11:9.note: The Hebrew word for "searched" is the same one used for mining silver in Proverbs 2:4, painting the sage as a prospector digging for wisdom's ore.

11:10.rendering: The words of the wise are like goads, and like nails firmly fixed are the collected sayings, given by one Shepherd.
11:10.note: The Hebrew pairs "goads" that prod the reluctant with "nails" that fasten truth in place, both coming from a single Shepherd whose voice echoes through the sages.

11:11.rendering: But beyond these, my son, be warned: of making many books there is no end, and much study is a weariness of the flesh.
11:11.note: The Hebrew word for "study" (lahag) implies anxious muttering or obsessive absorption, a wry warning that endless scrolls can exhaust the body without yielding true wisdom.

11:12.rendering: The end of the matter, everything having been heard: fear God and keep his commands, for this is the whole of humanity.
11:12.note: The Hebrew word for "whole" (kol) echoes back to the book's opening "vanity of vanities, all is vanity," quietly answering that the fear of God gathers up every scattered piece of life under the sun.

11:13.rendering: For God will bring every deed into judgment, including every hidden thing, whether good or evil.
11:13.note: The Hebrew word for "deed" (ma'aseh) echoes the book's opening refrain that there is nothing new under the sun, pulling the whole search for meaning under the gaze of the coming Judge.
